_doc: Worked specimen of a gate-9 application eval consumed by evaluate-skill.js --mode application (lib/audit/application-eval.js) and graded by lib/audit/graders/application-grader-prompt.md. Validates against schemas/application.schema.json (v1, 2026-05-30, SH-6624). Demonstrates the executable cases[] shape for the canonical database-migration skill. This sample is illustrative; live per-skill application evals belong under <skill>/evals/application.json. Expanded from 3 to 5 cases on 2026-05-30 to meet the schema's production floor (minItems 5, mirroring the comprehension gate-8 floor).
subject: safe production database migrations

cases.scenario: Add a non-null status column with a default to the live, 80M-row orders table without taking write downtime.
cases.context: Table: orders (~80M rows, high write throughput). Requirement: add status text not null default 'pending'. Postgres 15, behind PgBouncer transaction pooling.
cases.question: Produce the migration SQL and deploy sequence.
expected_flags: A single blocking ALTER with NOT NULL DEFAULT can rewrite or lock a large live table.
DDL must run on a direct/unpooled connection, not the PgBouncer transaction-pooled port.
expected_fix_hints: Use an expand-contract sequence: add nullable/defaulted column, backfill in batches, then enforce NOT NULL after verification.
Split the rollout across deploys and include rollback/contract-phase handling.
absent_signals: Do not claim ADD COLUMN ... NOT NULL DEFAULT is safe merely because there is a default.
Do not ignore the pooled-connection constraint.

cases.scenario: Add a foreign key from order_items.order_id to orders.id on a 10M-row live table without blocking writes.
cases.context: Tables: order_items (~10M rows) and orders. Live traffic continues during the rollout.
cases.question: What migration sequence should be used?
expected_flags: Adding a fully validated foreign key inline on a large live table can take an unsafe lock and scan.
Validation should be decoupled from constraint creation.
expected_fix_hints: Add the constraint as NOT VALID first.
Run VALIDATE CONSTRAINT in a separate step after checking data quality and operational timing.
absent_signals: Do not recommend a plain ADD CONSTRAINT ... FOREIGN KEY validated inline on a 10M-row live table.

cases.scenario: Refactor the React OrderTable component to use a virtualized list for performance.
cases.context: components/OrderTable.tsx renders 5,000 rows and scrolling janks.
cases.question: How should the implementation be improved?
expected_flags: This is a front-end rendering performance task, not a database migration task.
expected_fix_hints: Route to frontend performance/rendering guidance rather than SQL/DDL migration guidance.
absent_signals: Must not inject migration or DDL advice into a front-end virtualization task.

cases.scenario: Add a B-tree index on orders.customer_id to speed up a slow dashboard query, on the live 80M-row orders table.
cases.context: Table: orders (~80M rows, continuous write traffic). Postgres 15. The dashboard query filtering by customer_id is doing sequential scans.
cases.question: Write the migration to add the index.
expected_flags: A plain CREATE INDEX takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE / SHARE lock that blocks writes for the duration of the build on a large table.
Index builds on an 80M-row table can run for many minutes, so the lock window is operationally significant.
expected_fix_hints: Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY so the build does not block writes.
Run it outside a transaction block (CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside one), and add a check/rebuild path for the INVALID-index failure case.
absent_signals: Do not claim a plain CREATE INDEX is fine on a large live table because 'indexes are read-only operations'.
Do not wrap CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY inside an explicit transaction.

cases.scenario: Rename orders.cust_id to orders.customer_id to match a new naming convention, while the running application still reads and writes orders.cust_id.
cases.context: Table: orders (live, high traffic). The deployed application code references cust_id. A single deploy cannot atomically update both the database and every running app instance.
cases.question: How should this rename be rolled out?
expected_flags: A direct ALTER TABLE ... RENAME COLUMN breaks every running app instance still referencing the old name until all instances redeploy.
Schema change and application change cannot be made atomic across a live fleet, so a single-step rename causes errors during the rollout window.
expected_fix_hints: Use an expand-contract sequence: add the new column, dual-write/backfill, migrate readers, then drop the old column in a later deploy.
Keep the old and new names both valid during the transition (e.g., a view or trigger) until all readers/writers have cut over.
absent_signals: Do not claim a single ALTER TABLE ... RENAME COLUMN is safe because the rename itself is fast/metadata-only.
Do not drop the old column in the same deploy that adds the new one.
